mechanism_narrative: A month-over-month decline in FEDFUNDS signals easing financial conditions, lowering discount rates and supporting equity multiples. When policy rates are falling, S&P 500 returns over the next month should be positive relative to periods with stable or rising rates.
failure_condition: Reject if 20-day forward SPX returns are not higher after 3-month declines in FEDFUNDS than after flat or rising FEDFUNDS, or if the triggered mean return is negative in a majority of rolling 3-year windows.

mechanism_narrative: A rise in UNRATE above its recent trend marks labor-market deterioration and rising recession risk. That signal should precede negative S&P 500 returns over the following month as earnings expectations and risk appetite weaken.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises by at least 0.3 percentage points over 3 months
failure_condition: Reject if UNRATE-rise triggers are followed by positive 20-day SPX returns more often than negative returns, or if the conditional mean return is nonnegative across two consecutive 3-year evaluation windows.

mechanism_narrative: A positive monthly acceleration in RSAFS indicates improving nominal consumption and revenue growth, which should lift equity earnings expectations and support near-term S&P 500 returns. The signal is strongest when sales growth is broad-based rather than a one-month spike.
failure_condition: Reject if positive RSAFS momentum does not correspond to higher 20-day SPX returns than negative or flat RSAFS momentum, or if the sign of the conditional effect flips in any rolling 4-quarter sample.

mechanism_narrative: When INDPRO is rising while CPIAUCSL is not accelerating, output growth is expanding without an inflation shock, which should be equity-positive because margins and demand expectations improve without forcing tighter policy. This mechanism predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next quarter.
failure_condition: Reject if joint INDPRO-up and CPIAUCSL-flat/down states do not outperform all other states on 60-day SPX returns, or if the interaction term is insignificant and unstable across rolling 5-year windows.

mechanism_narrative: A FEDFUNDS level above 5% indicates restrictive policy that compresses valuation multiples and raises the probability of earnings downgrades. After this threshold is crossed, S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days should be negative.
failure_condition: Reject if SPX 20-day returns are positive more often than negative when FEDFUNDS exceeds 5.0%, or if the conditional average return is not below the unconditional baseline in backtests.

mechanism_narrative: A decline in HOUST signals weakening housing demand and slower transmission into consumption, employment, and credit growth. If housing starts fall from the prior month, S&P 500 returns over the next week should be negative as cyclical risk reprices quickly.
failure_condition: Reject if month-over-month HOUST declines fail to precede negative 5-day SPX returns more often than not, or if the effect is absent after controlling for the recent level of HOUST.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous decline in PAYEMS and rise in UNRATE identifies labor-market weakening more clearly than either series alone. That combination should predict negative S&P 500 returns over the next month because weaker payroll growth and higher unemployment jointly imply deteriorating earnings momentum.
failure_condition: Reject if joint PAYEMS decline plus UNRATE increase does not produce lower 20-day SPX returns than either signal alone, or if the combined state yields positive average returns in more than 40% of rolling 2-year windows.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-shock recovery, upward revisions or sustained gains in GDPC1 imply stronger real demand and better corporate revenue prospects, supporting S&P 500 returns over the next quarter. The signal should be strongest after periods of weak GDP growth rather than during mature expansions.
failure_condition: Reject if positive GDPC1 momentum does not precede higher 60-day SPX returns than negative GDPC1 momentum, or if the effect disappears once the sample excludes recession-to-expansion transitions.
